title: Week 2 Lecture Slides (Measuring Social Impact, TOC amp; Logic Model)
body:
Dr Linh Nguyen, Course Convenor Lecturer UNSW Centre for Social Impact Email: linh-nguyen@unsw.edu.au   Acknowledgement of Country

We acknowledge the Traditional Custodians of the lands where we currently live, work and play. We pay our deep respects and give thanks to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Elders past and present for their custodianship of Country.  Social Impact

Social Impact Measurement

Theory of Change & Logic Model

Plan for Week 3  ▪ Assist you in understanding, measuring and demonstrating social impact

▪ Introduce you to social impact measurement approaches and case studies  Please share your own definition of social impact  • “The intended and unintended social consequences, positive and negative, of programs (interventions, policies, plans, projects) and any social change processes invoked by these” (Ramia et al 2021)

• “The net effect of an activity on a community and the well-being of individuals and families… A significant, positive change that addresses a pressing social challenge.”(Center for Social Impact)

• “Significant or lasting changes’ in people’s lives, brought about by a given action or series of actions” (Roche 1999)

• "Social, non-financial impacts of programmes, organisations and interventions, including, for example, the wellbeing of individuals and communities, social capital and the environment" (Wood and Leighton 2010)    Significant Lasting Changes

Interventions/ Improvements Well-being actions  Who might be interested in learning about the social impact of social enterprises?  Discussion

Who might be interested in learning about the social impact of social enterprises? • The enterprises themselves? • Their employees? • The supporters (investors, donors, intermediaries, and others) • Clients? • The government? • The society/ communities?    defining, monitoring, and employing measures   Communicating Purpose & Inspiring Action: a strategy to communicate the organisation’s purpose, engage communities ,and inspire action

“Demonstrating the benefits of our people having a job is super important. And it's not just IndigiGrow, it's all organisations that have programs that try to achieve good in the in the wider community”

Challenging Stereotypes & Addressing Racism: Sharing measurable impact helps educate the broader community and break down barriers.

“It makes it easier for someone like me to actually educate the community, educate the wider community about what it is or why we even exist as an organisation. That's when we start to really have impacts around breaking down stereotyping, breaking down barriers between non-Aboriginal people and Aboriginal people. Because they can then see the good that we are doing and what we are trying to do”. Peter Cooley Building Credibility & Securing Funding: Impact measurement is central to organisational credibility and Founder and CEO IndigiGrow sustainability to attract funding “We're moving into a stage now where organisations really have to be able to explain their impact or demonstrate their impact in order to give them the best chance of succeeding with future funding.”  Social impact measurement is very very important. It’s closely linked to my business strategy and plan. It is also necessary because I can know if I should continue these activities, these approaches or should I change them. Do my activities bring benefits to the kids as we want? The measurement could show us what we should do and what we should adjust. Ms Ngan Pham, Co-Founder of Tohe, 2014 Before we make decision to invest, social impact is obviously an important part of due diligence process. We always try to identify something similar like the theory of change ... we really want to understand what problems social enterprises are trying to solve, are they really addressing it, is it scalable. We then identify specific metrics we would like to measure, that’s social impact. And in that way we might be able to tell a more compelling growth story of the enterprise . Ms Shuyin Tang – Partner at Paramatar Capital & Former Regional Director LGTVP, 2014, Co-founder and CEO of the Beacon Fund, a boundary-breaking fund for female entrepreneurs in Southeast Asia’s one of Australia’s “100 Women of Influence” by the Australian Financial Review  Why measure the impact of social enterprise?  Stakeholder Benefits Organization • Organisational learning: know you’re making a real difference (social • Enhance performance & impact enterprise/ • Direct resources effectively social impact • Stakeholder dialogue: inspire and motivate staff, trustees, funders, the business ) government and other stakeholders • External accountability; societal legitimacy/credibility • Marketing; attract customers/clients • Appease investors/funders; attract funding Funder • Understand the difference funders make, directly and indirectly. • Learn from what they, and those they fund, do • Ensure they make the most of their resources Public/ • Inform best practices Community • Contribution to broader impact • Advocate policy

Source: Based on Ebrahim and Rangan (2014), Muir,and Bennett (2014); Rawhouser et al (2019)   Stakeholder roles and needs Identify all relevant stakeholders

Where do they fit into the program/ initiative/ organisation and measurement process?

What do they bring?

What are their needs?

Prioritise stakeholder needs and levels of Who is key to the journey, and engagement. Consider how important it is to include and what do they need to know? engage different stakeholder groups in the measurement process

What to Measure – Levels of Measurement

Macro • Societal level (e.g. population, government, industry, sector data)

• Organisational level

Meso (e.g. NGO, Enterprise, CSR, social business data) • Community level • Individual (e.g. changes

Micro to people, households and families) • Program (e.g. data on an individual program or initiative)

How to measure: Multiple Frameworks

INFORMAL ….. APPROACHES    Theory of Change Logic Model

Definition ▪ An explicit theory or model of how a program will achieve the • A visual representation of how a program will achieve its goals, intended or observed outcomes. It articulates the hypothesised including the short-, medium- and long-term outcomes. causal relationships between a program’s activities and its • It comprises a detailed representation of inputs, activities, intended outcomes and identifies how and why changes are outputs, outcomes and impact (Ramia et al 2021, p 18). expected to occur (Ramia et al 2021, p 16). ▪ The theory of change comprises a change model (the changes the program intends to achieve) and an action model (the activities that will lead to those changes) Focus Explains why and how change will happen. It delves into the Focuses on what will happen. It lays out the logical steps of a underlying causes and mechanisms that connect activities to program, showing the connection between resources, activities, outcomes. and expected outcomes. Complexity More nuanced, contextualized. It acknowledges there might be More linear. It shows a clear cause-and-effect relationship between multiple pathways to achieving the desired outcome and considers activities and outcomes. potential challenges.

Development ▪ Can be developed before a program begins to inform design ▪ Can be created at the start, or after a program is designed to stage/timing and identify the best interventions. clarify its intended flow and to communicate its goals and ▪ Often starts with the long-term goal and works backward. activities to stakeholders. ▪ Moves forward to identify expected outputs and outcomes.

In essence, a logic model can be part of a well-developed theory of change. The Theory of Change provides the overall vision and rationale, while the Logic Model breaks down this vision into manageable and measurable components.   ▪ Inputs are the necessary resources for a program to run. E.g. staff, volunteers, funding, buildings, technology, machinery. ▪ Activities & Processes are what the program is doing and how we do it. E.g. online information, webinars. ▪ Outputs are numbers or counts of things that result from the program. E.g. number of online webinars, number of participants. ▪ Outcomes are the direct changes that your program produces in the short-, medium-, and long-term. ▪ Impact is the lasting, systemic change to which your program or organisation contributes.

Source: Ramia et al (2021, p 18)   ULTIMATE OUTCOMES/ IMPACTS

MEDIUM-LONG MEDIUM-LONG TERM MEDIUM-LONG Time frame TERM OUTCOMES OUTCOMES TERM OUTCOMES

SHORT TERM SHORT TERM SHORT TERM OUTCOMES OUTCOMES OUTCOMES

OUTPUTS OUTPUTS OUTPUTS

INPUTS/ INPUTS/ INPUTS/ ACTIVITIES ACTIVITIES ACTIVITIES  Theory of Change – DIY kit https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6zRre_gB6A4   • Impact reporting and investment standards (IRIS): https://iris.thegiin.org/ • Social Return on Investment (SROI): https://socialvalueuk.org/ • Global Reporting Initiative (GRI): https://www.globalreporting.org/standards • Outcome Star: https://outcomesstar.com.au/  • •
• •
▪ Indicators are the markers that show whether change has occurred in an underlying condition or circumstance. Indicators can be expressed as percentages, proportions, numbers, ratios, or perceptions, behaviours, satisfaction, quality (Ramia et al 2021, p.25) ▪ Impact indicators are data points that reflect the results of programs and initiatives in changing the conditions, behaviours, or well-being of individuals and communities (Salamon 2014)  Impact Indicators Cultural Preservation ▪ Number of cultural workshops/events/learning activities held ▪ Number of participants engaging in cultural learning activities ▪ Qualitative feedback on cultural identity, pride, or connection Biodiversity & Conservation ▪ Number of native species planted or restored ▪ Area of land regenerated or protected (hectares) ▪ Increase in habitat quality or wildlife sightings ▪ Participation in sustainability training or awareness activities  Indicators are measures that show whether progress is being made on individual outcomes or goals Qualitative evidence Quantitative evidence • Seek to understand how the world is • Seek to explain facts by using understood; help to unpack the ‘why’ numerical data: how many, how much, how often. • Interpreted and experienced by individuals, groups and organizations (usually through • They are highly structured and the eyes of the people being studied and in based on theory/ evidence natural settings). • Usually objective, but they can also • Often richly descriptive, flexible, relative capture subjective responses such and subjective. as attitudes and feelings

Qualitative methods vs. Quantitative methods Qualitative methods Quantitative methods • participant observation • surveys • interviews • control trials • focus groups • cohort studies • document and policy analysis • experimental design studies • ethnography and • analysis of datasets. observation, • participatory methods. Analysis approach: thematic Analysis approach: Data can be analysis, document and policy analysed through descriptive analysis, triangulation, network statistics, econometrics, statistical analysis, case study and narrative methods, mathematical modelling analysis. and predictions.  Benefits of Mixed Methods (MM) QUALITATIVE QUANTITATIVE + DEPTH BREADTH

Qualitative method Quantitative method • How were changes experienced by • How many? individuals? • How much? • What actually happened on the • How representative of the ground? population? • The quality of the services/ • Are changes statistically significant? products • The quality of life and well-being

• No single approach can fully explain how the programs/ interventions operate in the real world • Programs/ Activities operate in complex and changing environments • Programs/ Activities are affected by multiple contextual factors (economic, social, cultural, political, historical, etc) • Programs / Activities may change depending on the sector and target population respond • MM can combine the BREADTH of Quantitative methods and the DEPTH of Qualitative methods   OOMPH: What to measure & demonstrate  OOMPH https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iQX8VNXS0vg   Educators report deep impact on their teaching

Educators enjoy teaching more  ▪ Collecting and reporting on outputs are much easier and more achievable. However, qualitative data might be difficult ! ▪ Data can be poor, inappropriate, incorrect or misleading ▪ Timing issues ▪ Resources and strategies: how can measurement be aligned with budgets, actions and decisions of the organization? ▪ Being lost: Which tools and/or approaches provide a best fit for what needs to be measured? What are the most appropriate indicators? Should ‘immeasurable’ outcomes be measured?

Source: CSI (2014); Ramia et al (2021)   It is true that “you can’t mange what you can’t measure” yet also true that “not everything that counts can be counted, and not everything that can be counted counts”. Jed Emerson Blended Value Indicators vary from one project to another, even the same project undertaken in different contexts will need to adapt measurement indicators accordingly. As we hear repeatedly “one size does not fit all” Pamela Hartigan Skoll Centre for Social The goal now is not to have one accepted Entrepreneurship University of Oxford measurement tool embraced by all. It will never happen due to the diversity of visions and missions that drive the work of philanthropy Boug Bauer The Clark  ▪ Complex: involve multiple actors and factors ▪ Unstable: independent of project duration ▪ Non-linear: unexpected, emergent, continuous, intervention may change ▪ Beyond control: subject to influence ▪ Incremental, cumulative  • There is no ideal approach/ tool • Can combine multiple approaches • The decision is up to you and should be based on: • Your motivation • Your capacity and resources • Your strategy • Your readiness  ▪ Ebrahim, A., & Rangan, V. K. (2014). What Impact?: A Framework for Measuring the Scale and Scope of Social Performance. California Management Review, 56(3), 118–141. https://doi.org/10.1525/cmr.2014.56.3.118 ▪ Muir, K., & Bennett, S. (2014). The compass: Your guide to social impact measurement. The Centre for Social Impact. ▪ Nesta. (2014, January 16). DIY toolkit | Theory of change [Video]. YouTube. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=6zRre_gB6A4 ▪ Oomph! Wellness. (2015). Annual impact report 2014/2015 [PDF]. Care Management Matters. https://www.caremanagementmatters.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/2015/09/Oomph-Annual-Report-1415.pdf ▪ Ramia, I., Powell, A., Stratton, K., Stokes, C., Meltzer, A., & Muir, K. (2021). Roadmap to outcomes measurement: Your step-by-step guide to planning, measuring and communicating social impact. Centre for Social Impact. ▪ Rawhouser, H., Cummings, M., & Newbert, S. L. (2019). Social Impact Measurement: Current Approaches and Future Directions for Social Entrepreneurship Research. Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice, 43(1), 82–115. https://doi.org/10.1177/1042258717727718 ▪ Roche, C. J. (1999). Impact assessment for development agencies: Learning to value change. Oxfam. ▪ Salamon, LM 2014, Leverage for good : an introduction to the new frontiers of philanthropy and social investment, Oxford University Press, New York. ▪ Impact reporting and investment standards (IRIS): https://iris.thegiin.org/ ▪ Social Return on Investment (SROI): https://socialvalueuk.org/ ▪ Global Reporting Initiative (GRI): https://www.globalreporting.org/standards ▪ Outcome Star: https://outcomesstar.com.au/  ▪ Review Client Project Brief ▪ This video COMM3030 - Conversation with Peter Cooley (captioned).mp4 provides background on IndigiGrow and highlights the importance of social impact measurement and demonstration in supporting its mission. ▪ Visit to IndigiGrow (Thursday 23d October, Week 6): "Discovering Native Plants and Bush Foods Nursery Tour" at La Perouse Nursery Registration deadline: 3pm Friday 26th September via IndigiGrow Visit: Discovering Native Plants and Bush Foods Nursery Tour

This is an optional activity that provides a great opportunity for experiential learning and group project. We can offer up to two students per group due to space constraints. Please discuss with your teammates about your plan to join the tour. There will be 2 sessions on this date. Transport will be provided. Students will be collected and dropped off at UNSW Gate 2, with a bus organized by CSI. Session 1 ▪ Tour Time: 10:30 AM – 12:00 PM ▪ Students be at UNSW Gate 2: by 9:50 AM ▪ Bus Departure: 10:00 AM Session 2 ▪ Tour Time: 1:00 PM – 2:30 PM ▪ Students be at UNSW Gate 2: by 12:20 PM ▪ Bus Departure: 12:30 PM You can express your preferred session and we will do our best to accommodate your preference. However, the final allocation will depend on various factors and we may not always be able to meet individual requests. Thank you for your understanding and flexibility.  Please find more information via this link: Apply to the new Centre for Social Impact UG Leadership Fellowship, valued at over $10,000! | UNSW Current Students

Applications close: Tuesday 30 September 2025. Download the application form here.

Live Lecture: First Client Meeting • Research the organization, the project brief & ask questions • Project Management & Consulting (Pre-recorded) • Action Research & Data (Pre-recorded) • Key Methods of Collecting Primary Data (Pre-recorded) Workshop • Project Check-in • Project Management & Consulting • Action Research & Data • Review the Client project description and assessment description. • Discuss with your teammates and Tutor about your individual research theme for Assessment 1  THANK YOU
